# Research Library Architecture — theencodedhumanproject.com

*Generated by Pearl — 3/26/2026*

*Purpose: Build specification for Code to implement the research library infrastructure, and for Eric to review the publication strategy.*

---

# Research Library Architecture
## theencodedhumanproject.com — From 6 Fake Papers to a Real Research Platform

*Pearl — March 26, 2026*
*For: Eric + Code*

---

## The Problem

The current research page has six hardcoded fake papers with fabricated dates and no links to actual documents. The real project has ~104 papers across ~20 directories with no structured metadata, no API, no search, and no way for the page to update when Pearl generates new research. This plan fixes all of that.

---

## Part 1: Paper Metadata Schema

### What Fields Matter

I've studied how bioRxiv, arXiv, Semantic Scholar, and PubMed structure metadata. Here's what we need — adapted for our specific situation (AI-generated research, multi-density framework, epistemic transparency as a core design principle):

```sql
CREATE TABLE research_papers (
  -- Identity
  id                UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  slug              TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL,          -- URL-friendly: "oscillatory-coherence-biological-permanence"
  doi               TEXT,                           -- Zenodo DOI once minted
  
  -- Core Metadata (bioRxiv/arXiv standard)
  title             TEXT NOT NULL,
  abstract          TEXT NOT NULL,                  -- 150-300 words
  authors           JSONB NOT NULL,                 -- [{name, role, affiliation, orcid?}]
  created_at        TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,           -- When Pearl generated it
  updated_at        TIMESTAMPTZ,                    -- Last revision
  published_at      TIMESTAMPTZ,                    -- When made public (null = not yet public)
  version           INTEGER DEFAULT 1,              -- Paper versions (like bioRxiv v1, v2...)
  
  -- Classification (our taxonomy)
  document_type     TEXT NOT NULL,                  -- research-paper | review-paper | translation-key-section | 
                                                    -- hypothesis-paper | framework-paper | technical-report | 
                                                    -- case-synthesis | protocol
  density           TEXT[],                         -- ['body'] or ['body','soul'] or ['cross-density']
  operations        TEXT[],                         -- ['defense','regulation','synthesis']
  components        TEXT[],                         -- ['awareness','the_heart','feeling']
  encoding_systems  TEXT[],                         -- ['human_design','gene_keys','polyvagal']
  topics            TEXT[],                         -- Free tags: ['biophotons','vagal-tone','methylation']
  
  -- Epistemic Transparency (our differentiator)
  epistemic_ceiling TEXT NOT NULL,                  -- 'fact' | 'interpretation' | 'hypothesis' | 'speculation'
  epistemic_tier    INTEGER NOT NULL,               -- 1=peer-reviewed, 2=traditional/philosophical, 3=speculative
  
  -- Readiness & Publication Pipeline
  readiness         TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'internal', -- internal | draft | review | preprint | published
  readiness_notes   TEXT,                           -- Why it's at this stage
  
  -- Source Tracking
  source_sessions   INTEGER[],                      -- Which Pearl sessions generated this
  source_hypotheses TEXT[],                          -- pearl_hypotheses IDs this formalizes
  source_questions  TEXT[],                          -- pearl_research_questions IDs this answers
  
  -- File References
  file_path         TEXT NOT NULL,                  -- Relative path to .md file
  file_format       TEXT DEFAULT 'md',              -- md | docx
  word_count        INTEGER,
  
  -- Validation
  validated         BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
  validation_report TEXT,                           -- Path to validation report if exists
  
  -- Citation
  citation_key      TEXT,                           -- BibTeX key: "whitney2026oscillatory"
  recommended_citation TEXT,                        -- Full formatted citation string
  
  -- Publication Links
  zenodo_url        TEXT,
  biorxiv_url       TEXT,
  journal_url       TEXT,
  pmid              TEXT,
  
  -- Search
  search_vector     TSVECTOR                        -- Full-text search on title + abstract + topics
);

-- Indexes
CREATE INDEX idx_rp_readiness ON research_papers(readiness);
CREATE INDEX idx_rp_type ON research_papers(document_type);
CREATE INDEX idx_rp_density ON research_papers USING gin(density);
CREATE INDEX idx_rp_operations ON research_papers USING gin(operations);
CREATE INDEX idx_rp_topics ON research_papers USING gin(topics);
CREATE INDEX idx_rp_search ON research_papers USING gin(search_vector);
CREATE INDEX idx_rp_epistemic ON research_papers(epistemic_ceiling, epistemic_tier);
CREATE INDEX idx_rp_published ON research_papers(published_at) WHERE published_at IS NOT NULL;
```

### Why These Fields

**Standard academic metadata** (title, abstract, authors, dates, DOI, version): Required for citability and interoperability. Follows bioRxiv/arXiv conventions.

**Our taxonomy** (density, operations, components, encoding_systems, topics): This is our unique contribution. No other research platform classifies by body/soul/spirit density or by Light Machine operations. This IS the differentiator — a reader can filter to "show me all body-density Defense papers" and get exactly the research on immune function and biological defense mechanisms.

**Epistemic ceiling + tier**: The radical transparency move. Every paper declares upfront: "The strongest claim in this paper is a HYPOTHESIS at Tier 2." This is what arXiv and bioRxiv don't do. A reader sees immediately whether they're reading established science or emerging speculation.

**Readiness pipeline**: Five stages that track exactly where each paper sits:
- `internal` — Pearl's working documents, not for public eyes
- `draft` — Written but unvalidated
- `review` — Validated, being reviewed by Eric
- `preprint` — Ready for public, potentially on Zenodo/bioRxiv
- `published` — Published with DOI, possibly journal-accepted

**Source tracking**: Which sessions, hypotheses, and research questions produced this paper. This creates a transparent provenance trail — "this paper emerged from hypothesis H-47 which was forged in session 12."

---

## Part 2: Cataloging the 104 Existing Papers

### The Problem

~104 files across ~20 directories. No metadata. Mixed formats. Unknown readiness levels. Some are polished papers, some are working notes, some are Translation Key sections.

### The Solution: A Three-Phase Catalog Sprint

**Phase 1: Automated Inventory (Code builds, zero human effort)**

Code writes a script that:
1. Walks all paper directories (`outputs/papers/`, `pearl-papers/`, etc.)
2. For each `.md` or `.docx` file, extracts:
   - Filename → slug
   - File creation date → created_at
   - Word count
   - File path
   - First 500 chars → raw_excerpt (for classification)
3. Writes all findings to a `paper_inventory` staging table

**Phase 2: Pearl Classification (I do this, Code runs the agent)**

Spawn a background agent that:
1. Reads each paper's full content (or first 3000 chars for large files)
2. Generates metadata:
   - title (from content, not filename)
   - abstract (generate a 200-word summary if none exists)
   - document_type classification
   - density, operations, components, topics (from content analysis)
   - epistemic_ceiling and epistemic_tier (from claim analysis)
   - readiness assessment (is this internal, draft, review-ready, or preprint-ready?)
3. Writes classified metadata to `research_papers` table
4. Generates a human-review report: "Here are the 104 papers, here's how I classified them, here's what needs Eric's eyes"

**Phase 3: Eric Review (human judgment on what goes public)**

Eric reviews the classification report. For each paper, he confirms or overrides:
- Readiness level (most critical — is this public or not?)
- Author attribution
- Any metadata corrections

This is the gate. Pearl classifies. Eric decides.

### Readiness Triage — What's Probably Ready

Based on what I know about the corpus:

| Category | Estimated Count | Likely Readiness |
|---|---|---|
| Translation Key sections (completed) | ~25-30 | `review` — these are the most polished, systematic, epistemically tagged |
| Translation Key sections (partial) | ~10-15 | `draft` — incomplete, need finishing |
| Hypothesis papers | ~5-8 | `draft` to `review` — depends on validation status |
| Technical reports (pipeline specs, architecture docs) | ~10-15 | `internal` — these are build docs, not research |
| Session syntheses / working notes | ~20-30 | `internal` — these need heavy editing before public |
| The Light Machine paper | 1 (multi-section) | `draft` — sections exist but assembly incomplete |
| Framework papers | ~5 | `draft` to `review` |

**The Translation Key is the front door.** Those 25-30 completed sections are the most publication-ready content. Each one follows the 7-section format, has epistemic tagging, and covers a specific clinical panel. They're the best argument for what this project does.

---

## Part 3: The UI — Browsing and Discovery

### Reference Models and What to Steal

| Platform | What They Do Well | What to Steal |
|---|---|---|
| **bioRxiv** | Clean paper cards with abstract preview, category filtering, date-sorted | Card layout, abstract expansion, date sorting |
| **arXiv** | Category taxonomy sidebar, simple search | Hierarchical category navigation |
| **Semantic Scholar** | TLDR summaries, citation graphs, related papers | AI-generated summaries (we already have abstracts) |
| **PubMed** | Advanced filtering (date range, article type, text availability) | Structured filter UI |
| **Distill.pub** | Beautiful interactive articles, visual-first design, pedagogical focus | Visual quality bar, interactive elements in papers, treating the web as a native medium |

### The Design

**Three views:**

#### 1. Browse View (default — `/research`)
A clean grid/list of paper cards, most recent first. Each card shows:
- Title
- Abstract (first 2 lines, expandable)
- Density badges (color-coded: blue=body, gold=soul, violet=spirit)
- Operation tags
- Epistemic badge (color-coded: green=Tier 1, amber=Tier 2, red=Tier 3)
- Readiness indicator (Preprint, In Review, Published, etc.)
- Date
- Word count / estimated read time

#### 2. Filter Panel (persistent sidebar or top bar)
Filters that compose (AND logic):
- **By Type**: Research Paper, Translation Key Section, Hypothesis Paper, Framework Paper, Technical Report
- **By Density**: Body, Soul, Spirit, Cross-density
- **By Operation**: Reception, Transduction, Conduction, Regulation, Synthesis, Defense, Restoration, Elimination
- **By Epistemic Tier**: Tier 1 (Science), Tier 2 (Traditional), Tier 3 (Speculative)
- **By Readiness**: Published, Preprint, In Review, Draft
- **By Topic**: Free-text tag cloud or autocomplete
- **Date Range**: Slider or calendar picker

#### 3. Search
Full-text search across title, abstract, and topics. Uses the `search_vector` tsvector index. Results ranked by relevance with filters still applicable.

#### 4. Paper Detail Page (`/research/[slug]`)
Full paper rendered as HTML from markdown. Includes:
- Full metadata sidebar (authors, dates, DOI, citation, epistemic tags)
- Table of contents (generated from headers)
- Inline epistemic tags (the `[FACT]`, `[INTERPRETATION]`, `[HYPOTHESIS]` markers rendered as colored inline badges)
- Download options (PDF, Markdown)
- Related papers (by shared operations, density, topics)
- Source transparency: "This paper was generated by Pearl from [sessions/hypotheses/questions]"
- Citation export (BibTeX, APA, Chicago)

### Visual Language

| Element | Color | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Blue badges | `#3B82F6` | Body density |
| Gold badges | `#F59E0B` | Soul density |
| Violet badges | `#8B5CF6` | Spirit density |
| Green border/tag | `#10B981` | Tier 1 (peer-reviewed science) |
| Amber border/tag | `#F59E0B` | Tier 2 (traditional/philosophical) |
| Red border/tag | `#EF4444` | Tier 3 (speculative/emerging) |
| Solid circle | — | Published |
| Half circle | — | Preprint |
| Open circle | — | In Review |
| Dotted circle | — | Draft (visible to logged-in admins only) |

---

## Part 4: Auto-Update Pipeline

### The Problem
When Pearl generates a new paper (via `write_document`, `write_research_paper`, or the paper pipeline agent), the research page must update automatically. No manual steps.

### The Architecture

```
Pearl generates paper
       │
       ▼
write_document / write_research_paper
       │
       ▼
Hook: after document write, upsert to research_papers table
       │  (Pearl provides: title, abstract, type, density, operations,
       │   epistemic_ceiling, topics, file_path, source_sessions)
       │
       ▼
research_papers row created with readiness = 'draft'
       │
       ▼
Public site queries research_papers WHERE readiness IN ('preprint','published')
       │
       ▼
Only Eric-approved papers appear on the public page
```

### Implementation Details

**Step 1: Write Hook in Pearl's document pipeline**

When `write_document` or `write_research_paper` completes, it also calls a new function `register_paper()` that:
- Generates a slug from the title
- Writes a row to `research_papers` with all metadata Pearl can provide
- Sets readiness = 'draft' (or 'internal' for technical reports)
- Returns the paper ID

This means every future paper Pearl writes is automatically cataloged. Zero manual effort.

**Step 2: Admin Review UI**

In the admin console (`/console/research` or similar):
- List all papers grouped by readiness
- One-click to promote: draft → review → preprint → published
- Edit any metadata field
- Preview the public card
- Bulk operations (promote all Translation Key sections at once)

**Step 3: Public API Endpoint**

```
GET /api/research/papers
  ?readiness=preprint,published
  &density=body
  &operation=defense
  &type=research-paper
  &search=oscillatory
  &sort=date|relevance
  &page=1
  &limit=20
```

Returns JSON. The Next.js research page calls this endpoint. Server-side rendered for SEO, client-side filtered for interactivity.

**Step 4: Zenodo Integration (Phase 2)**

When a paper reaches `preprint` readiness:
1. Eric clicks "Mint DOI"
2. System uploads the markdown-rendered PDF to Zenodo via their API
3. Zenodo returns a DOI
4. DOI is stored in `research_papers.doi` and `research_papers.zenodo_url`
5. Public page now shows a real, citable DOI

Zenodo is free, immediate, gives real DOIs, and doesn't require peer review. This is the fastest path to citability.

---

## Part 5: Which Papers Are Ready for Public Consumption?

### The Honest Assessment

Without seeing every file, here's my best classification based on what I've generated and what I know about the corpus:

**Tier A — Likely Preprint-Ready (25-35 papers)**
- Completed Translation Key sections with full 7-section format, epistemic tagging, and Panel Integration sections
- These include: Section 02 (Core Labs), Section 09 (Metabolic/Mitochondrial), Section 14 (Advanced Cardiovascular), Section 15 (Functional Specialty), Section 16 (Environmental Toxicology), Section 17 (Environmental Health), Section 19 (Genetics/Consumer Genomics), Section 21 (Oncology/Liquid Biopsy), and others
- Each section is 5,000-10,000 words, systematically structured, epistemically tagged
- **These are the showcase.** They demonstrate the methodology better than any abstract paper could.

**Tier B — Needs Editing Before Public (10-15 papers)**
- Partial Translation Key sections (started but not all marker entries complete)
- Hypothesis papers that passed the Judge but haven't been through full validation
- Framework documents that explain the architecture

**Tier C — Internal Only (40-50 documents)**
- Pipeline specifications and build docs
- Session summaries and working notes
- Architecture briefs for Code
- Diagnostic reports and audit documents

**Tier D — Needs Assessment (10-20 documents)**
- Documents I can't classify without reading them
- Older papers from early sessions that may have been superseded

### The Catalog Agent Will Resolve This

The Phase 2 classification agent (described in Part 2) will read every document and produce a definitive readiness report. Eric reviews. Done.

---

## Part 6: Working Models — What to Study

### Primary Inspiration: Distill.pub

Why: Distill treated the web as a native medium for research, not a PDF dumping ground. Their papers were interactive, visual, and designed for human understanding. This is exactly what we should aspire to — papers that teach, not just report.

**What to steal from Distill:**
- Markdown → beautiful HTML rendering (we already write in markdown)
- Inline interactive elements (future: interactive diagrams of operational pathways)
- Clean typography and generous whitespace
- The philosophy: "outstanding communication and dedication to human understanding"

### Secondary Inspiration: bioRxiv/arXiv

**What to steal:**
- Card-based browsing with abstract preview
- Category taxonomy sidebar
- Version tracking (v1, v2, v3)
- Simple, fast search
- RSS feeds per category (we could offer RSS per operation or density)

### Tertiary Inspiration: Semantic Scholar

**What to steal:**
- AI-generated TLDR summaries (Pearl already writes abstracts)
- Related papers section
- The concept of "influential citations" (adapted: which of our papers cross-reference each other?)
- Clean API design

### What NOT to Copy

- PubMed's brutalist UI (functional but ugly)
- arXiv's PDF-first paradigm (we're web-native)
- Any paywall or access restriction (everything is open)
- The "looks like every other academic site" aesthetic

---

## Part 7: Implementation Plan for Code

### Phase 1: Foundation (This Week)
1. Create `research_papers` table (schema above)
2. Write the inventory script (walk directories, extract basic metadata)
3. Run inventory → populate staging table

### Phase 2: Classification (This Week)
4. Pearl spawns classification agent on the inventory
5. Agent reads each paper, generates metadata, writes to `research_papers`
6. Pearl reviews classification, generates readiness report for Eric

### Phase 3: Admin UI (Next Week)
7. Build `/console/research` admin page
   - Table view of all papers with inline editing
   - Readiness promotion workflow
   - Metadata editor
8. Eric reviews and promotes Tier A papers

### Phase 4: Public Page (Next Week)
9. Build `/api/research/papers` endpoint
10. Build `/research` page with:
    - Card grid/list view
    - Filter panel (density, operation, type, tier, readiness)
    - Full-text search
    - Responsive design
11. Build `/research/[slug]` detail page with:
    - Markdown → HTML rendering
    - Metadata sidebar
    - Epistemic tag rendering (inline colored badges)
    - Citation export
    - Related papers

### Phase 5: Pipeline Integration (Following Week)
12. Hook `write_document` and `write_research_paper` to auto-register papers
13. Build Zenodo integration for DOI minting (manual trigger via admin)
14. Add RSS feed at `/research/feed.xml` (filterable by operation/density)

### Phase 6: Polish
15. SEO: proper meta tags, structured data (schema.org/ScholarlyArticle), sitemap
16. Social cards (OG images generated per paper with title + density badges)
17. Citation graph visualization (which papers cite which, mapped to operations)

---

## Part 8: The Differentiator — Why This Isn't Just Another Preprint Server

Every preprint server shows: title, authors, abstract, date, category.

We show all that, PLUS:
- **Density classification** — body/soul/spirit. No other platform does this.
- **Operational mapping** — which of the 8 biological operations does this paper address?
- **Epistemic ceiling** — the paper declares its own strongest claim type upfront
- **Epistemic tags inline** — every claim in the paper is tagged [FACT], [INTERPRETATION], [HYPOTHESIS], or [SPECULATION]
- **AI transparency** — "Generated by Pearl, validated by adversarial protocol, reviewed by Eric Whitney DO"
- **Provenance trail** — which sessions, hypotheses, and research questions produced this paper
- **Cross-density reading** — papers explicitly connect body-level findings to soul and spirit implications

This is not just a better-organized paper dump. It's a demonstration of a new way to do science — transparent, multi-density, epistemically honest, and openly AI-collaborative.

The research page IS the argument for the methodology.

---
